metadata.title: Quick-fire: Truth across different rooms
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Companion to qf-truth-001. Whereas qf-truth-001 varied counterparty across a fixed in-person register (housemate, friend, customer-service, senior, anonymous survey, subordinate), this round varies modality and audience structure (digital channel, group, application form, child, brief in-person, family-confidence). Same binary forced-choice format under 8-second timer. Provides a second probe per session that triangulates qf-truth-001 — if a user is consistently honest across both, that's stronger signal than within-round consistency alone.
metadata.domain_anchor: Abeler et al. 2019 Econometrica honesty-meta (calibration); Hofmann et al. 2014 Science (everyday-modality coverage matters more than dramatic dilemmas); Vrij 2008 detection-of-deception work on channel effects.

items.prompt: You realize you accidentally forwarded an internal email to someone outside the company an hour ago. The content isn't disastrous but isn't meant for them.

options.text: Wait and see if anything comes of it.

items.prompt: In a meeting, someone misattributes a quote to you that you didn't actually say. The misattribution makes you sound smarter than the actual conversation.

options.text: Speak up: "Actually I didn't say that — that was J."
Let it pass; you can correct later if it comes up.

items.prompt: A job application form asks how proficient you are in a skill where you'd describe yourself honestly as "moderate." The form's options are: "Basic," "Proficient," "Expert."

options.text: Pick Basic — closer to honest than Proficient would be.
Pick Proficient — the question doesn't really capture nuance and Basic undersells you.

items.prompt: A child you're close to (around 7 years old) asks about something the adults in their life have been actively avoiding telling them about. You're in the kitchen alone with them.

options.text: Tell them, age-appropriately, what you actually know.
Redirect — "That's something for your parents to talk about with you."

items.prompt: A stranger at a party brings up a much-discussed recent book. They assume you've read it. You haven't, but you've absorbed the gist from podcasts.

options.text: "I haven't actually read it, but I've heard a lot about it."
Engage on the topic; you know enough to keep up.

items.prompt: A close family member asks if their adult child — your cousin — is "doing ok." You know your cousin is struggling but they confided in you in confidence.

options.text: "That's something to talk to them about directly." (don't reveal; don't pretend either)
